Data and
Jour          nalism:
Histor           y and
Development

A brief        histor      y of
data jour     nalism



       What are the roots to data journalism?
       Data journalism is grounded in a larger
       quantitative turn within journalism. (Petre,
       2013)
       Current wave of quantitatively oriented
       journalism has deep democratic roots
       (Coddington, 2014: 2).
       Related to the practice of computer-assisted
       reporting (CAR)
       CAR branched off  to  include multiple related
       practices;
       Computational journalism, open-                                                                                                                                                        source
       journalism, and data  journalism.                                                          Image Source:
                                                                                                  http://datajournalismhandbook.org/1.0/en/c
                                                                                                  ase_studies.html

W   hat is data
jour     nalism?




      In short, data journalism is about the
      process of filtering the flow  of data and
      creating new  stories out of raw  sets of
      information.
      “Data can be the source of data journalism,
      or it can be the tool with  which  the story is
      told — or it can be both. Like any  source, it
      should be treated with scepticism; and like
      any tool, we  should be conscious of how  it
      can shape and restrict the stories that are
      created with it.”
      (Paul  Bradshaw,   in The Data Journalism
      Handbook, 2012)





                                                                         Image Source: http://datajournalismhandbook.org/

Dealing with data


An Approach to Data Journalism

Histor           y and
Development

Histor      y and
backg  r ound



     Bogost et. al. (2010) traces the visual
     representation of data from the 1930s
     onwards.
     From maps, to economic and statistic charts
     and graphs to more contemporary
     “chartoons                    ”.
     Chartoons were popularised by Nigel
     Holmes in the 1960s and 1970s
     The shift from static print infographics to
     interactive data visualisations.

Nigel Holmes on history of
information graphics

Infog   r   a  phics:
A brief            Over           view

Reading
infog  r  a phics



       Data visualisations  can be traced back to
       traditional  forms of storytelling – from written
       to visual narrative.
       Segal and Heer                                                                                                                                                (2010) describe seven
       genres of narrative visualization: magazine
       style, annotated chart, partitioned poster,
       flow  chart, comic strip, slide show,  and
       video.
       The focus of this lecture is primarily
       infographics and interaction data
       visualisation.

Se  gal and                    Heer                                                                                                                                                                                                                Genr es of       nar    r  ative

                       (1)                      genre,
                       (2)                       visual narrative tactics,

Char      tjunk






        Chartjunk is a term used by statistician
        Edward Tufte (in Dick, 2016) to describe
        redundant data or visual elements that to
        not convey  meaning to the audience.
        A good data visualisation should not contain
        chartjunk.

                         Functionalist                               -
                         idealist



Concerned with communicating the truth
through data
Visualisations  as primarily  a scientific
methodology – visual logic
More common in statistics and maths
Doesn’t account for contested ideas of what
‘the truth’ is

                         Pr  a gmatist-
                         Realist



           The idea that infographics are just a tool or a
           technology – rather than communication
           Considers the practical realities of publishing
           an infographic – where  it is being published,
           and what the constraints of the medium are

                     Summarising
                     Standar  ds



                                                           These categories  may  overlap. For example
                                                           – a functionalist infographic may still be
                                                           playful  for example


                                                           These categories  to two  things:

                                                           Secondly, categorise specific audiences and
                                                           intention within  infographic production

                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                           24

                    Summar      y






                                                        Journalism has undergone recent shifts,
                                                        incorporating data into journalistic practices
                                                        Data visualization  and infographics are one
                                                        form of this
                                                        Journalism and data stories are just one
                                                        way  in which  ‘data’ is communicated to a
                                                        wider audience
                                                        There is no set global  or industry  standards
                                                        of infographics, but rather a series of ways
                                                        we  can frame data, design, and
                                                        communication
